FMCG Demand Planning & Data Validation System
From inconsistent data to confident decisions
________________________________________
Overview
This project demonstrates how to generate reliable, actionable demand planning decisions from imperfect FMCG data.
Instead of assuming clean inputs, the system:
•	Validates data before use
•	Identifies inconsistencies
•	Introduces an operational correction loop (recounts)
•	Re-evaluates data reliability after intervention
•	Produces outputs that can be acted on with confidence
________________________________________
The Problem
In real business environments, planning data is rarely perfect.
This dataset included:
•	Inconsistent product identifiers
•	Multiple pack sizes distorting movement
•	Formatting issues (scientific notation, decimals)
•	No clear product hierarchy
•	Movement data that did not reconcile cleanly
This creates a critical issue:
A correct calculation can still lead to a wrong decision.
________________________________________
Approach
Rather than forcing perfect data, I designed a system around control and validation:
1.	Standardise movement into cases
2.	Rebuild product grouping logic
3.	Validate movement behaviour
4.	Classify reliability (Acceptable / Check / Problem)
5.	Isolate problem records
6.	Introduce recount process
7.	Apply corrected stock values
8.	Recalculate planning
9.	Revalidate outcomes
________________________________________
System Flow
Raw Data  
→ Standardise  
→ Validate  
→ Flag Issues  
→ Recount  
→ Adjust Stock  
→ Recalculate  
→ Revalidate  
→ Decision
________________________________________
Dashboard Outputs
________________________________________
1. Data Health Overview
Shows:
•	Total SKUs
•	% Acceptable
•	% Check
•	% Problem
Current state:
•	Acceptable: 63.64%
•	Check: 25.00%
•	Problem: 11.36%
________________________________________
2. Current Data Reliability
Visual distribution of data quality:
How much of the dataset can be trusted right now?
________________________________________
3. Priority Actions
A focused action layer showing:
•	Product (with pack size clarity)
•	Suggested order
•	Days of cover
•	Data reliability status
This functions as a working decision tool, not just a report.
________________________________________
4. Impact of Recount Process
Tracks improvement after intervention:
Status	Before	After
Acceptable	27.27%	63.64%
Check	42.05%	25.00%
Problem	30.68%	11.36%
Improvement: +36.36% reliable data
________________________________________
Key Design Principles
•	Do not plan on data you cannot trust
•	Separate data issues from stock issues
•	Preserve original data (no overwrites)
•	Apply corrections as a layer
•	Validate results after action
•	Prioritise usability over complexity
________________________________________
Development Reality
This project was not a straight build.
Challenges included:
•	Structured reference behaviour in Excel
•	Named ranges vs tables conflicts
•	Data type mismatches breaking lookups
•	Formula debugging across dependent layers
•	Balancing clean logic with usable outputs
Several parts were:
•	built
•	broken
•	tested
•	rebuilt
This reflects how real systems are developed.
________________________________________
Timeline
Built as an iterative working session:
Phase	Activity
1	Data cleaning & structuring
2	Product grouping logic
3	Validation layer
4	Debugging and restructuring
5	Recount process design
6	Feedback loop integration
7	Dashboard build
8	Final validation & refinement
________________________________________
What This Demonstrates
•	Demand Planning capability
•	Data validation under imperfect conditions
•	Operational thinking (not just analysis)
•	Ability to build a working correction process
•	Decision-making under uncertainty
________________________________________
Final Thought
This is not a perfect model.
It is a realistic one.
It shows how to move from:
Inconsistent data → Controlled decisions → Measurable improvement
________________________________________
Key Takeaway
Reliable decisions do not require perfect data — they require control, validation, and the ability to act on what can be trusted.
